ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for Petha specifically; mapped to the nearest confection food product class. Petha is a sugar-crystallized ash gourd candy unique to Indian cuisine.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Sugar confectionery) is the closest defensible match. No specific Petha entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Petha or ash gourd confectionery. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Petha is approximately 50 grams, equivalent to one or two medium-sized pieces of the translucent sugar-crystallized ash gourd candy.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Petha is not listed as a standalone entry in NIN tables. Values are adapted from NIN ash gourd (winter melon) raw data combined with standard sugar syrup confectionery composition, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for candied fruit/vegetable confections (FDC ID 169655, Candied fruit). The high carbohydrate and sugar values reflect the heavy sugar syrup processing. Saturated fat estimated at 0.0g given the fat-free nature of the product confirmed by multiple Indian food composition references.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sugar Confections and Candied Vegetable Products — analogous estimate based on sucrose-dominant confectionery
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Petha exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated by analogy to sucrose-dominant confectionery and candied fruit products (GI ~65–80) listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant carbohydrate in Petha is refined sucrose (~65g/100g), which drives a high GI response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 35.0) / 100 = 26.3 for a 50g serving (35g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (10 mg/100g); fat-free; no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Petha is naturally very low in sodium and fat, making it unlikely to directly worsen blood pressure; however, its very high sugar content contributes to excess caloric intake and weight gain, which are indirect risk factors for hypertension, so portion control is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high refined sugar content (65g/100g) and high glycemic index (GI ~75)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Petha should be strictly avoided or consumed only in very small amounts by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as its very high refined sugar content and high glycemic index (GI ~75) will cause rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (65g/100g) contributing to hypertriglyceridemia and excess caloric intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Petha is fat-free and cholesterol-free, its very high free sugar content (65g/100g) can raise serum triglycerides and contribute to weight gain, both of which are cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar intake is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: High refined sugar consumption is associated with increased systemic inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimize or avoid Petha to reduce inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Petha is a celebrated traditional Indian confection originating from Agra, Uttar Pradesh, and is one of the most iconic sweets associated with the city of the Taj Mahal. It is made exclusively from ash gourd (Benincasa hispida, also known as winter melon or white gourd), which is peeled, deseeded, and thoroughly pricked before being boiled in a concentrated sugar syrup until the pieces become translucent, soft, and crystalline. The finished product has a delicate, mildly sweet flavour with a soft, slightly chewy texture and a jewel-like translucent appearance. Petha can be served in a dry crystallized form or in a syrupy form. It is one of the very few fat-free traditional Indian sweets, making it distinct from ghee-rich mithai. Popular regional variants include Angoori Petha (small grape-sized pieces in syrup), Kesar Petha (saffron-infused), and Paan Petha (stuffed with betel leaf filling). Nutritionally, Petha is very high in refined sugar (approximately 65g/100g) and carbohydrates (approximately 70g/100g), providing around 285 kcal per 100g, with negligible protein (0.4g) and fat (0.1g). Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those on low-sugar diets. It is low in sodium (10 mg/100g) and cholesterol-free. Petha is a popular souvenir and gift item for visitors to Agra and is widely available across North India.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of Petha?
What is ash gourd sweet called?

confidence_reason: Petha is not listed as a standalone entry in the NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods tables or USDA FoodData Central with an exact match. Nutritional values are adapted from NIN ash gourd raw composition data combined with standard sugar confectionery processing adjustments, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central candied fruit/vegetable entries. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on sucrose-dominant confectionery. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from NIN (ICMR) ash gourd data and USDA FoodData Central candied confection entries; no exact standalone Petha entry found in any single approved source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Petha not available in NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; the product is effectively fat-free (total fat 0.1g/100g) making fatty acid fractions analytically negligible. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the finished Petha product not reported in available approved sources. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-dominant confectionery in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Petha was found. 4. Saturated fat reported as 0.0g consistent with the fat-free nature of the product. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed negligible/absent in ash gourd and sugar-based confections per NIN and USDA references). 5. Portions in the input JSON contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 50g. 6. This is a North Indian regional dish; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Hindi and Tamil local names are provided as the dish has pan-Indian recognition.
